questions.q: What is the main difference between publish-subscribe (pub-sub) and point-to-point messaging?
options: Pub-sub broadcasts messages to multiple subscribers; point-to-point delivers a message to a single consumer
Pub-sub is synchronous while point-to-point is asynchronous
Pub-sub guarantees message ordering while point-to-point does not
Point-to-point messaging requires a central broker while pub-sub does not
questions.explanation: Pub-sub enables one-to-many messaging to all subscribers; point-to-point is one-to-one. Option 1 confuses synchronization with messaging patterns. Option 2 is false; pub-sub doesn't guarantee ordering. Option 3 is incorrect; point-to-point systems also use brokers.

questions.q: What is an 'at-most-once' delivery guarantee in message queues?
options: A message is delivered zero or one time; it will not be redelivered even if delivery fails
A message is guaranteed to be delivered within one network round trip
Only one instance of a consumer can process messages from the queue
The queue holds at most one message before starting to drop new messages
questions.explanation: At-most-once means no duplicates but possible loss. Option 1 conflates timing with delivery semantics. Option 2 concerns the number of consumers. Option 3 is about queue capacity.

questions.q: What does 'backpressure' mean in message queue systems?
options: A mechanism to slow down producers when consumers cannot keep up, preventing queue overflow
Pressure exerted by failed messages being retried repeatedly
The delay caused by network congestion between brokers
A signal sent by the broker to drop low-priority messages
questions.explanation: Backpressure is flow control that regulates producer rate to match consumer capacity. Option 1 is about message failure, not pressure. Option 2 is network latency. Option 3 describes message dropping, not flow control.

questions.q: What is 'exactly-once' semantics in message delivery and why is it challenging to achieve?
options: Each message is processed exactly once despite failures; it requires coordination between producer, broker, and consumer with idempotent operations
The broker ensures messages are delivered to exactly one broker node at a time
Each consumer receives exactly one copy of every message published
Exactly-once semantics is trivial to implement using standard TCP guarantees
questions.explanation: Exactly-once requires deduplication and coordination across the entire system with idempotent operations. Option 1 concerns broker replication, not delivery. Option 2 assumes all consumers must receive all messages. Option 3 underestimates the complexity.

questions.q: What role does a message broker play in a message queue system?
options: It receives messages from producers, stores them durably, and delivers them to consumers according to routing rules
It acts as a transparent proxy that directly connects producers to consumers
It enforces strict ordering of all messages across all partitions globally
It provides computation services to transform messages before delivery
questions.explanation: A broker decouples producers and consumers with durable storage and routing. Option 1 assumes transparency without storage. Option 2 is false; brokers don't guarantee global ordering. Option 3 is not a core broker function.

questions.q: What is a message partition in queue systems like Kafka?
options: A subset of messages in a topic that preserves order within the partition, enabling parallel consumption
A separation of message types that prevents mixing of different payload formats
A network segment that isolates consumer groups from producers
A compression algorithm that splits large messages into smaller chunks
questions.explanation: Partitions enable ordered processing and parallelism; each partition is an ordered log. Option 1 confuses type separation with partitioning. Option 2 describes isolation, not partitioning. Option 3 is about compression.

questions.q: What is a consumer group in Kafka-like systems?
options: A set of consumers sharing the same subscription that collectively process all partitions of a topic
All consumers in a network that communicate with a single broker
A temporary group of consumers that form during a network partition
A priority tier that determines which messages consumers receive first
questions.explanation: Consumer groups enable distributed consumption where partitions are divided among members. Option 1 assumes all consumers share the same broker. Option 2 is temporary grouping, not a consumer group. Option 3 is about priority, not grouping.

questions.q: What is the difference between message retention policies?
options: Retention policies determine how long messages are stored (time-based or size-based) before deletion
Retention policies dictate which consumers can access messages
Retention policies ensure messages are never deleted from the broker
Retention policies control the network bandwidth used for message transmission
questions.explanation: Retention policies manage message lifetime in storage. Option 1 concerns access control, not retention. Option 2 is false; messages are deleted per policy. Option 3 is about bandwidth, not storage duration.

questions.q: What does 'offset' mean in the context of message queue consumers?
options: The position in a partition's log indicating which messages have been consumed, enabling resumption after failures
A delay added to message delivery to prevent overwhelming consumers
The unique identifier assigned to each message in the queue
A calculation of latency between producer and broker
questions.explanation: Offsets track consumption position; storing offsets enables exactly-once replay. Option 1 is a delay mechanism. Option 2 is a message ID, not an offset. Option 3 is a latency metric.

questions.q: Why do message queues typically use durable storage rather than in-memory buffers?
options: Durable storage persists messages across broker failures and enables consumer recovery from any point in the log
In-memory buffers are always faster, but durability is a secondary feature
Durability prevents consumers from processing messages out of order
Durable storage automatically guarantees exactly-once delivery semantics
questions.explanation: Durability ensures data survives failures and allows replay. Option 1 assumes speed is the primary concern. Option 2 is false; durability doesn't enforce ordering. Option 3 is a misconception; durability alone doesn't guarantee exactly-once.
